english: len. — lag-mtíl (lag mthil) the palm of the hand. — lag-dám (lag dam) Mil., lag-dám-po (lag dam po) C. close-fisted, stingy, niggardly. — lag-dar (lag dar) Lex., prob. the same as láb-dár (lab dar) (W. col.) grater. — lag-γdúb (lag gdub) bracelet. — lag-bdé (lag bde) Mil., C., the person that pours out the tea at a tea-carousal. — lág-dón (lag don) Cs. a vassal or subject paying his landlord in money or kind, opp. to rkaṅ-gró (rkang gro) who performs his services as an errand-goer or a porter. — lag-rdúm (lag rdum) Mil. having a mutilated or crippled hand. — lag-ldán (lag ldan) having a hand or a trunk, hence = elephant, Cs. — lag-brdá (lag brda) sign or signal made by the hand, beckoning. — lag-na-rdó-rǰe (lag na rdo rje), lag-rdór (lag rdor) v. rdó-rǰe (rdo rje). — lag-na-γžoṅ-togs (lag na gzhong togs) Cs. 'holding a basin in his hand', n. of a deity. -- lag-snód (lag snod) = lag-tíg (lag tig). -- lag-dpón (lag dpon) work-master, overseer, esp. builder Dzl., Glr. — lag-ṕyis (lag phyis) a piece of cloth for wiping the hands, towel, napkin. — lag-búbs (lag bubs) v. búb-pa (bub pa). lag-bér (lag ber) walking-staff. — lág-mí (lag mi) bail, surety. — lag-dmár (lag dmar) C. hangman. — lag-btsúg (lag btsug) shoot, scion. — lag-tsígs (lag tshigs) joint of the hand, wrist; elbow-joint. — lag-γzúṅs (lag gzungs), W. *lag-zúm* (*lag zum*), balustrade, banister, railing. — lág-γyog-pa (lag g.yog pa) companion, assistant, associate. — lag-rís (lag ris) the lines in the palm of the hand Sch. — lag-lén (lag len), resp. ṕyag-lén (phyag len), Sch. also lag-stabs (lag stabs), practice, practical knowledge, dexterity, Cs.: čós - kyi (chos kyi) lag - lén (lag len) the practice of religion, khrims-kyi (khrims kyi) of the law, rtsís-kyi (rtsis kyi) of mathematics. — lag-śúbs (lag shubs) glove.

resp. and eleg. for yin-pa (yin pa) and _gyúr-ba ('gyur ba), to be; lágs-so (lags so) like yin (yin), as answer to a question: so it is! yes to be sure! very well! at your service! When a Lama asks a shepherd: kyéd-kyi (khyed kyi) miṅ (ming) ći (ci) yin (yin) what is your name? the latter answers: N. N. byá-ba (bya ba) lags (lags) my name, if you please, is N.N., and asks on his part: blá-ma (bla ma) kyed (khyed) ći (ci) skad (skad) byá-ba (bya ba) lags (lags) what may be the name of your Reverence? Mil. — de (de) kyed (khyed) lágs-sam (lags sam) is it you, Sir? Pth.; dge-sloṅ (dge slong) de (de) su (su) lags (lags) who is this reverend gentleman? Dzl.; ći (ci) ltar (ltar) lags-pa (lags pa) (for gyur-pa (gyur pa)) γsol-pa (gsol pa) he reported (to Buddha) what had happened, Dzl.; blá-ma-la (bla ma la) bžugs-grogs (bzhugs grogs) med-pa (med pa) lags-sam (lags sam) Mil. has your Reverence no attendant? ltá-ba (lta ba) ma (ma) lágs-kyi (lags kyi) that does not mean: to behold, but . . . Dzl.; _oṅ-ba ('ong ba) ći (ci) lags (lags) 'what is it that this comes here?' i.e. how does this happen to come here? Glr.; rǰe (rje) ći (ci) lags (lags) what is that, Sir? (when one is surprised at any thing strange or unaccountable, at an unreasonable demand etc., also when we should say: God forbid!) Glr.; yin (yin) lags (lags), yda (yda) lags (lags), yod (yod) lags (lags) there is, it is Glr.; žal-zás (zhal zas) γsol (gsol) lags-nas (lags nas) when we shall have done dining Dzl.; a Lama asks: btsal-le (btsal le) (= btsal-lam (btsal lam)) have you looked for it? and the disciple answers: btsal (btsal) lags (lags) yes, I have! Mil.; in addressing a person: blá-ma (bla ma) lags (lags) (prop: you that are a Lama) for the mere vocative case, ὦ ἱερεῦ, Mil., frq. -- In W. lags (lags) is not in use now (cf. however le (le) 3), but in C. it is of frq. occurrence, e.g. in Lhasa: *lā* (*la*), *lā-so* (*la so*), *lā yo'* (*la yo'*), *lā yin* (*la yin*) for: yes, Sir! very well, Sir! *lā?* (*la*) *lā-am?* (*la am*) *lā-sam?* (*la sam*) please? what did you say?

Ceylon, laṅ-ka-pu-ri (lang ka pu ri) city of the Rakshasas in Ceylon, which island is the abode of these beings, according to the belief of many people in Tibet and northern India even at the present day; laṅ-kar (lang kar) gśegs-pai (gshegs pai) mdo (mdo) the Sutra Laṅkāvatāra in the Kangyur.
weak, e.g. from hunger, disease Ld.
Scopolia praealta Don., a common weed with pale yellowish flowers Med.; in Lh. a species of Hyoscyamus, of frq. occurrence, seems to be understood by the same name.

(provinc. lóṅ-ba (long ba)), pf. laṅs (langs), imp. loṅ(s) (long(s)), = ldaṅ-ba (ldang ba), I. to rise, to get up, da (da) loṅs (longs) get up now! also with yar (yar) (pleon.); laṅs-te (langs te) sdod-pa (sdod pa) to stand, Lt. and col; to arise, e.g. of a contest W., C.; to go away, to depart, esp. fig., of the night: nam (nam) láṅs-te (langs te) at daybreak; to come forward, to step forth, from among the crowd Do.; ṕyír (phyir) láṅ-ba (lang ba) to recover, to be restored, to grow well, to come to one's self, after a faint-
